C[Phelan: The best I could do to still get eyespace in the side was C7, which is uncomfortably small.

C[Phelan: Try to avoid the ""Empty Triangle""(C10,D10 and D11) as much as you can. It rarely is a good shape to play.
Phelan: A better move would have been at C12.
C[Phelan: F11 seems a good move, but it leaves a cutting point at F10, as you later saw. you should have fixed it by playing F10 at this point.
C[Phelan: I assume this next sequence of black moves tried to separate my upper corner from my lower corner. I don't think it was possible to do so at this point.
C[Phelan: Here's an empty triangle again.

C[Phelan: At this point, your center stones are getting in trouble, since I have two ataris that are also cutting points, F10 and H7. You should have played at one of them, since capturing my D6 stone wasn't as urgent.
Phelan: Of F10 and H7, F10 is more important, since you don't have eyes in that corner yet. F10 would not only help connect, but would also give you an eye at E11.
C[Phelan: C12 was unnecessary for me, since your group could only get one eye by playing there.

C[Phelan: I think your main problem in this game was focusing too much on the center, and trying to cut my groups before connecting yours.
It was an interesting game, though. :)

C[Phelan: Though this move encircles my center stone, it leaves your stones separated, which is usually bad on a 9x9.
C[Phelan: They are more connected now, but the one point jump between them leaves them vulnerable to cuts.
C[Phelan: This was both to avoid being surrounded, and to keep my stones connected.

C[Phelan: This is a solid response, since it mostly secures the lower left corner for you. The only problem with it is that it still leaves the possible cuts at D5 and E6. I would have played D5 instead.
C[Phelan: This was a good move, you saw that playing at E7 would leave you too fragile. This move abandoned the F6 stone and kept your stones mostly connected.
C[Phelan: This stone was a sacrifice to leave your upper shape more fragile, and use the cut at C6 later
C[Phelan: Here is your ""abandoned"" stone still at work, I had to defend or be cut.

C[Phelan: I guess you already saw from the game what I'm about to tell you: your groups here are still fragile, and can be cut. Defending at C2 would probably be better here. G2, the cut, is a risky move, and it depends on whether black can live on the right side, and whether white can live or kill something on the left.

C[Phelan: I thought I could confine and kill your left group easily, but had forgotten about your ""abandoned"" stone at F6. It could allow you to scoop out my territory, and live on the right. I had to defend from that.

C[Phelan: This would have been better one stone lower, at H4, threatening an extension towards or invasion of my top corner. It would also almost guarantee the life of the black group, since it would only need J2 to live after that.

C[Phelan: Saving the F6 stone wasn't important, you could afford to sacrifice it. Closing the lower right corner would be the best. Second best would be to connect at C4, to attempt to kill my C2 group before I killed yours.

C[Phelan: The D4 group is now captured, (this is a frequent shape), and your top group is now becoming threatened. Like I said before, you could afford to sacrifice the F6 stone. It had already did its job.

C[Phelan: This is an interesting move. By threatening to cut, it can usually steal a vital point and kill a group. However, here I already have one eye at D4, so I can afford to just get one eye in the top corner. Saving the lower left corner, or connecting at B5 would be much better.

C[Phelan: This is a nice combination, if you're willing to fight(or can win) the ko at F9. However, this ko was much better for me than for you: If I win it, I can capture part of your left corner group, and leave very fragile
column2: If you win, you just take a stone, and a bit of my corner territory, but my group is still strong, and can still live.

column1: C[Phelan: This would be beter at B7, connecting. I could have played at B7 myself, and fight the ko if you captured at C5.
C[Phelan: I already commented on this situation on the message I sent you. I misread this corner, and playing at J2 would save it, since the usual move of J3 doesn't make one of the eyes false.

C[Phelan: I think I would prefer to play this one one stone lower, at C7, but I'm not sure if it would be better.
C[Phelan: I would prefer to play N4, or K6.
C[Phelan: There is no need to be this aggressive, since your groups are not under attack.
An attack from afar would be best, such as L3 or P3. L3 also has the advantage of starting to make a base for your K4 stone.
C[Phelan: Q3 would close up your corner better. Q2 would also work if you can deal with White R3 later.
C[Phelan: You should almost never allow this kind of thing to happen. See: http://senseis.xmp.net/?SqueezingOutTheToothpaste
C[Phelan: Good direction, but seems too close to G4. J3 would be better(threatening G2).
C[Phelan: H2 would more solid, and white would still have to answer it to avoid corner invasion.
C[Phelan: This was too deep, I think. A reduction like D13 seems better.
C[Phelan: When trying to escape, use moves that are harder to cut, such as one point jumps, simple extension, or a diagonal play.
C[Phelan: This was a White mistake, it should have been at L11.
C[Phelan: Again, another bad white move. L11 was much more important, as your next move showed.
C[Phelan: This was a great move. Although it can be cut, Black now has very strong influence towards the middle
C[Phelan: H10 should have been at H11, I think. Don't atari automatically.
C[Phelan: Very good move. I can't connect both sides.

C[Phelan: F12 looks better, since it is wider. That would give you better chances to make eyes. As soon as you get surrounded, you should start trying to get eyespace. If there is little eyespace, don't let yourself get surrounded.
C[Phelan: I think your only chance at life after F13 would be to threatean to cut at J16, and then split the white group at K17. I was worried that I wouldn't get time to play there first.
C[Phelan: This now has little chances of living. I think the best would be to try to close White's J9 group. L8, maybe? Even if white lives you get a strong center, and connect several groups.
C[Phelan: If you want to attack the N15 group, you should play one move ahead, at O14. That also helps get eyespace for the M13 group.
C[Phelan: O13 would be better here, I think. Sacrifices one stone, but gets better eyespace.
C[Phelan: O8 here would be better, I think. Again, it gets more eyespace.
C[Phelan: Black's inner group is still too weak for this cut. Always try to make your groups alive or strong before fighting.

C[Phelan: You played a good opening, I think. If you improve your fighting skills you should be able to get a lot stronger. Try to do more life and death problems, it should help visualizing which groups are safe and which are not.

C[rgatti: In Chess, opening with the Rook pawn is a lot like opening in Go with S2 or M9.  Maybe you can make something of those, but they are suboptimal and give your opponent significant positional advantages.

rgatti: People that memorize chess openings are missing the point.  But chess openings are what they are because of how they setup the board (White is trying to do something specific, and black is countering with something specific in mind)
rgatti: They are ""memorizable"" because, generally, deviating from them opens up vulnerabilities that a more skilled opponent can take advantage of

Phelan: a ladder means that you have a group of the opponent with two liberties, and you reduce it to one liberty. then, the opponent can only extend it to two liberties, and you can again reduce to 1, over and over
Phelan: there's a different type of ladder called loose ladder, which has the same principle, but instead of 1 and 2 liberties, you have 2 and 3.
Phelan: but to be a ladder, there has to be a sequence that one player can force
